coredata.dc:title: Solution processed graphene quantum dots decorated ZnO nanoflowers for mediating photoluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Young Jae
Ko, Kang Bok
Lee, Kyu Seung
Seo, Tae Hoon
Hong, Chang-Hee
Cuong, Tran Viet
Son, Dong Ick
coredata.dc:description: We report the fabrication of hybridization between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnO nanoflowers (NFs) for mediating photoluminescence by using a straightforward solution-based technique. Hydrothermally grown two-dimensional ZnO NFs played an essential role in assembling GQDs. The large surface area to volume ratio of ZnO NFs led to a highly dense distribution of GQDs, giving rise to photoluminescence (PL) emission at around 442 nm from GQDs and the synergic effects. Thus, GQDs/ZnO hybrid results in strong and broad PL over the spectral range from near UV to red region. We also suggested that an energy interaction between GQDs and various defect sites in ZnO NFs can promote the radiative recombination of both components, thereby providing PL peak intensities more than two times higher than individual components. Fabricated GQDs/ZnO heterojunctions on both soft and hard substrates emit similar intense and wide PL spectrum under ultraviolet irradiation, indicating that this heterojunction can consider as a promising platform for flexible and transparent optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
ZnO nanostructure
Heterojunction
Photoluminescence
Flexible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal growth of ZnO nanoflowers films
Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Korea Institute of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by financial support from the KIST Institution Program ( 2Z06030 ), Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( NRF-2017R1A2B3002307 & 2016M3A7B4900135 ), and Basic Science Research Program through the NRF of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1D1A3B03030758). We also acknowledge helpful advice from Prof. Russell Dean Dupuis at Georgia Institute of Technology (United States) and Prof. Jaehee Cho at Chonbuk National University (South Korea).

This work was supported by financial support from the KIST Institution Program (2Z06030), Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2017R1A2B3002307 & 2016M3A7B4900135), and Basic Science Research Program through the NRF of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1D1A3B03030758). We also acknowledge helpful advice from Prof. Russell Dean Dupuis at Georgia Institute of Technology (United States) and Prof. Jaehee Cho at Chonbuk National University (South Korea).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the hybrid of GQDs and ZnO NFs. (a) preparation of glass and PET substrates, (b) 5 nm-thick Al film deposition on the substrates using e-beam evaporator, (c) ZnO nanoflowers growth using hydrothermal growth method, (d) spray-coating of GQDs on the ZnO nanoflowers.

(a) SEM image of the surface of hydrothermally grown ZnO on dot-patterned Al on GaN, (b) enlarged SEM image of the R-area showing ZnO NRs, inset: cross-sectional SEM image, (c) enlarged SEM image of the B-area showing the boundary between ZnO NRs and NFs, inset: cross-sectional SEM image, (d) enlarged SEM image of the F-area showing ZnO NFs.
(a) HAADF image of GQDs on ZnO NFs, (b) EDS line profiling of GQDs on ZnO NFs.
XPS spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) GQDs on ZnO NFs.

(a) PL and absorption spectrum of GQDs, (b) PL spectra of GQDs, ZnO NFs, and GQDs/ZnO NF on glass substrates, (c) energy band diagram for the PL spectrum in GQDs/ZnO NFs, (d) PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs/ZnO on the PET substrates, Inset: Photograph of the GQDs/ZnO NFs on the PET substrate under illumination with 365 nm light-emitting diode.

head.ce:title: Solution processed graphene quantum dots decorated ZnO nanoflowers for mediating photoluminescence

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, 92 Chudong-ro, Bongdong-eup, Wanju, Jeollabuk-do 55324, Republic of Korea (D.I. Son). VKTech Research Center, Nguyen Tat Thanh University, 298-300 A Nguyen Tat Thanh Street, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam (T.V. Cuong).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the fabrication of hybridization between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnO nanoflowers (NFs) for mediating photoluminescence by using a straightforward solution-based technique. Hydrothermally grown two-dimensional ZnO NFs played an essential role in assembling GQDs. The large surface area to volume ratio of ZnO NFs led to a highly dense distribution of GQDs, giving rise to photoluminescence (PL) emission at around 442 nm from GQDs and the synergic effects. Thus, GQDs/ZnO hybrid results in strong and broad PL over the spectral range from near UV to red region. We also suggested that an energy interaction between GQDs and various defect sites in ZnO NFs can promote the radiative recombination of both components, thereby providing PL peak intensities more than two times higher than individual components. Fabricated GQDs/ZnO heterojunctions on both soft and hard substrates emit similar intense and wide PL spectrum under ultraviolet irradiation, indicating that this heterojunction can consider as a promising platform for flexible and transparent optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a kind of carbon-dots have received significant attention owing to their unique electrical, chemical, and optical characteristics [1,2]. Although graphene is a zero band-gap material, its functional groups located near the edge boundary have a specular band gap. Thus, the bandgap of graphene can be modulated by varying their size, shape, and bounded functional groups [3]. This artificial band-gap engineering provides attractive properties such as upconversion luminescence [4,5] and wide fluorescence ranges [6,7] from ultraviolet to visible region. Consequently, GQDs have emerged as excellent candidates for the fabrication of novel optoelectronic and bio-imaging devices owing to the quantum confinement effect [8]. However, so far, GQD preparation methods involving solution-based processes from organic materials have been limited owing to the hydrophilicity of these materials. GQDs have also been difficult to transfer onto substrates because of this characteristic. Here, we present a novel method to assemble densely packed GQDs onto both hard and flexible substrates, based on the straightforward fabrication of a hybrid nanostructure consisted of GQDs and two-dimensional ZnO nanoflowers (NFs). ZnO nanostructures have shown promising characteristics in the field of exciton-based optoelectronic devices owing to their high surface-to-volume ratio, elevated exciton binding energy (60 meV), and large direct band gap (3.3 eV) [9–11], combined with the presence of numerous defects originating from oxygen vacancies, zinc vacancies, and zinc interstitials. In this sense, excitons can be trapped in these defect sites, resulting in defect-related green–yellow emission. Recently, some groups have reported on hybrid nanocomposites ZnO structures such as ZnO-based core–shell structures [12], heterostructured nanocatalysts [13] and ZnO nanorod with graphene quantum dots [14]. Note that these heterostructures formed by different nanomaterials can present different features, thereby resulting in novel interface effects and phenomena and inducing new functional integrations for both materials [15]. In this study, two-dimensional ZnO NFs structures expected to be compatible with GQDs, facilitating energy transfer and giving rise to radiative recombination of GQDs and ZnO NFs. Among the various synthesis methods, a low-temperature solution-based process was applied because it enables the integration of different nanomaterials on both hard and flexible substrates while being compatible with several coating techniques. To demonstrate the roles of conjugation of GQDs to ZnO NFs, the structural and optical properties were studied using various techniques such as field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) measurement, scanning TEM energy dispersive spectroscopy (STEM-EDS), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal growth of ZnO nanoflowers films
ce:section.ce:para: The precursor solution for the hydrothermal growth of ZnO NF films was prepared by dissolving 1.2 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and 0.7 g of hexamethylenetetramine (HMT, C6H12N4) in 250 mL of deionized water. The mixture was mechanically stirred for 1 h at room temperature. As-prepared substrates with 5 nm-thick Al film were immersed in the mixture and heated to 95 °C for 2 h without any stirring. The sample was rinsed with deionized water and blew with nitrogen gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped GQDs dispersion was synthesized as follows: 0.84 g citric acid and 0.72 g ethylenediamine were slowly added into 20 mL water, and vigorously stirred until a homogeneous solution was obtained [16]. The as-prepared solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to the desired temperature of 160 °C with a heating rate of 1.5 °C/min and maintained for 5 h in a vacuum oven. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was discarded and the precipitates were collected, followed by re-dispersion in distilled water.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the fabrication of GQDs spray-coated on ZnO NF films. First, flexible and hard substrates such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and glass were cleaned by ultrasonication in ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, and deionized water for 20 min (Fig. 1a). Meanwhile, a 5 nm-thick Al film was deposited on both substrates by electron beam deposition (Fig. 1b). To further investigate the role of Al on the ZnO-NF film growth mechanism, the dot-patterned Al film (dot diameter: 500 μm) was prepared on a GaN film using a shadow mask. The GaN film was grown on a sapphire substrate by using a metal organic chemical vapor deposition system. The ZnO NFs were grown on the substrates in the prepared mixture (Fig. 1c). The ZnO NFs grown on the substrates were preheated at 110 °C on a hot plate for 3 min under air ambient. The solution-synthesized GQDs were then sprayed onto the ZnO NFs (Fig. 1d).

To thoroughly understand the effect of Al on the formation of ZnO NFs, ZnO nanostructures are grown by a hydrothermal growth method on a dot-patterned Al film on a GaN template. SEM images show the surface morphologies of the ZnO nanostructures prepared by the hydrothermal process. We aim to gain deep insight into the growth mechanism of ZnO on dot-patterned Al versus GaN to elucidate the role of the Al catalyst in the formation of the two-dimensional ZnO NF structures. Under identical growth conditions, the surface of the hydrothermally grown ZnO can be divided up into three distinct areas: (i) R-area attributed to ZnO grown on the GaN substrate, (ii) F-area ascribed to ZnO grown on dot-patterned Al, and (iii) B-area of ZnO grown at the GaN–Al dot boundaries. In fact, different ZnO nanostructure morphologies are observed at R-, B-, and F-areas, as illustrated in Fig. 2 b–d, respectively. As revealed by the higher-magnification SEM image in Fig. 2b, the hydrothermal growth achieves a structure comprised of straight nanorods (NRs) perpendicular to the (0001) GaN facet and highly uniform distribution across the entire R-area. The inset, showing a cross-sectional view of the ZnO NRs, confirms that nearly all single rods are perpendicular to the substrate. In general, the growth mechanisms of ZnO NRs have been discussed on the basis of both morphology evolution and hydrolysis process in the precursor solution [17–19]. Since both ZnO and GaN substrates have the same wurtzite crystal structure, phase separation can be ruled out. Moreover, the lattice mismatch between ZnO and GaN is only 1.9% along the c-direction. As a result, the hydrothermal growth forms vertical ZnO NR arrays along the [0 0 0 1] direction following the crystal orientation of the GaN template. Since the temperature increases in the reaction system during the hydrolysis process, HMT is first decomposed into formaldehyde and ammonia, which can be further converted into NH4 + and OH−. Here, we use HMT as a buffer to regulate the solution pH and control the supply of OH− species generated upon increasing the temperature. Meanwhile, Zn(NO3)2 can be oxidized to produce Zn2+ species on the surface, thus playing the role of a Zn precursor. These processes can be described by the following reactions:
(CH2)6N4 + 6H2O → 6HCHO + 4NH3
NH3 + H2O → NH4 + + OH−
Zn(NO3)2 → Zn2+ + 2NO3 −
Zn2+ + 2OH− → Zn(OH)2 → ZnO(NRs) + H2O

Fig. 2c shows a SEM image of hydrothermally grown ZnO nanostructures formed on the Al dot-pattern. We clearly observe two nanostructures in this area: NFs and NRs. Although ZnO nanostructures are hydrothermally grown on a GaN film under identical conditions, the ZnO NRs generated on a dot-patterned Al substrate show a different morphology (i.e., non-vertical form, as exhibited in the cross-sectional image in the inset of Fig. 2c) as compared to that shown in the inset of Fig. 2b. This result implies that the growth mechanism of ZnO NRs is influenced by the Al catalyst, resulting in non-vertical arrays. This phenomenon can be explained by additional reactions taking place upon the inclusion of Al(s) in the growth process. In this case, the effect of Al(s) on the growth mechanism of ZnO can be explained as follows. First, Al(s) would displace Zn by a single replacement reaction given the higher position of the former species in the reactivity series. As a result, Al(s) would be oxidized to produce Al(aq) 3+ species, which would serve as the main source of Al(OH)3 units for crystal growth via a combination of Al(aq) 3+ and OH−. Then, the Al(OH)3 species would undergo chemical reactions under superfluous OH− conditions in the presence of HMT to tentatively form anionic AlO2 − in solution. These processes are described by the following reactions:
2Al(s) + 3 Zn(NO3)2(aq) → 3Zn(s) + 2Al(NO3)3(aq)
Al(s) → Al3+ (aq) + 3e−
Al3+ (aq) + OH− → Al(OH)3 + OH− → AlO2 −AlO2 − ions would bind to positively charged Zn2+ species on the terminated (0 0 0 1) polar surface of ZnO more strongly than to another nonpolar surface. On the other hand, AlO2 − adsorption on the Zn2+-terminated faces results in the redistribution of the surface energy, thereby partially restricting the growth of ZnO along the [0 0 0 1] direction. Although the crystal growth still proceeds preferentially along the [0 0 0 1] direction, the growth rate ratio along this direction versus other crystallographic directions greatly decreases owing to the charge compensation effect of AlO2 − ions on the (0 0 0 1) surface, thereby allowing the lateral growth of ZnO NFs. In other words, the intrinsic growth of ZnO along the [0 0 0 1] direction to form ZnO NRs is substantially decreased, resulting in the formation ZnO NF structures instead, in virtue of the following reactions:
Z
n
2 +
+ 4 O
H
-
→ Zn
OH
4
2 -
→
AlO 2 -
Zn
O
( NFs )
As a result, as shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. S1, we exclusively observe ZnO NFs in the F-area via Al-assisted hydrothermal growth.

Fig. 3 show an HAADF image and EDS of the GQDs decorated on ZnO NFs, which was examined by a TECNAI G2 F20 S-twin (FEI, USA) TEM operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (AMETEK, USA). The synthesized GQDs with an average diameter of around 6 nm are randomly distributed on the surface of ZnO NFs (Fig. 3a and Fig. S2). As revealed by the electron diffraction pattern (Fig. 3a, inset), the GQDs exhibit good crystallization. The EDS line profiling analysis (Fig. 3b) confirms that the sample consists of C, O, and Zn. EDS line profiling is performed using an electron nano probe with a size of 1 nm over the sample. It can be concluded that a hybrid architecture of GQD-decorated ZnO NFs is formed via a spray-coating process.

The XPS spectrum is commonly used to analyze the components of the hybrid structure (Fig. 4 ). The XPS spectrum of GQDs reveals C1s (285 eV) and O1s (532 eV) peaks as shown in Fig. 4(a). Fig. 4(b) also shows the XPS spectrum of GQDs/ZnO NF, which consists of a Zn2p peak at 1022 eV. The weak shoulder peaks located at 499 eV and 476 eV correspond to Zn L3MM and Zn L2MM peaks, which indicates that relatively more zinc atoms are bonded to the oxygen atoms [20]. Different concentrations of carbon are found for GQD and GQD/ZnO NFs, as revealed by the C1s peaks of both samples. GQDs is barely deposited on hard substrates because of its hydrophilicity and small size (i.e., a few nanometers). Thus, this result implies that ZnO NFs can help GQDs to be captured on the surface. The high binding energy components of the O1s peak are usually attributed to the presence of loosely bound oxygen species on the surface, O2− ions on Zn2+, O2− ions in oxygen-deficient regions within the ZnO matrix, as well as with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid functionalities on GQDs.

In order to investigate the optical properties of GQDs and GQD/ZnO NFs structure, PL measurement was performed. Fig. 5 a shows the PL spectrum of GQDs under irradiation with a He-Cd 325 nm laser source at room temperature and UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO NFs grown on the glass substrate. The GQDs prepared herein show bright blue luminescence centered at 442 nm. The PL spectrum of GQDs overlaps with the absorption region of ZnO NFs, which is crucial for the energy transfer process. It has been reported that the size of GQDs can affect the energy gaps, resulting in various PL emissions from blue to brown [4,21]. One of our most interesting findings is PL behavior of the GQD/ZnO NFs structure. Fig. 5b shows the PL spectra of GQD, ZnO NFs, and GQD/ZnO NFs on glass substrates. When sprayed on a glass substrate, GQDs show a quite different PL spectrum as compared to that obtained when dispersed in solution. The PL intensity of the GQD/glass sample is significantly lower than that of GQD/ZnO NF/glass. This result indicates that GQDs are difficult to transfer to flat solid substrates or films. Inorganic nanoparticles such as GQDs based on powders are generally hard to make a good film formation on the surface. Since the two-dimensional ZnO NF have the large effective surface area, the density of GQDs/ZnO NF/glass can be even higher compared to GQDs/glass. The PL spectrum of the ZnO NF/glass is also shown in Fig. 5b. ZnO nanostructures generally exhibit ultraviolet (UV) and visible emissions corresponding to band-edge transitions and defect emissions originating from structural defects such as oxygen vacancies with different charged states, zinc vacancies, and zinc interstitial [22,23]. Emission from different types of defects in ZnO can show blue–green (480–550 nm), yellow (550–610 nm), and orange–red (610–750 nm) bands in the PL spectrum [23]. These different types of defects coexist in ZnO NFs, which shows a relatively weak UV emission at 379 nm and an asymmetric broad band ranging from blue to red regions. This defect emission may originate from the recombination of a photogenerated hole with an ionized charge state at the particular defects [24,25]. On the other hand, the GQD/ZnO NF/glass exhibit a strong broad PL emission centered at 520 nm. This broad band can be divided into various band emissions produced by the recombination from different defect levels. Interestingly, the PL spectrum of GQDs/ZnO NF is similar to that of the ZnO NF sample, except for the presence of an additional peak at around 425 nm, which may be produced by the GQDs. The PL stability of GQDs/ZnO NFs/glass was also investigated as shown in Fig. S3. The strong and broad PL spectrum of GQD/ZnO NF can be originating from a synergistic effect of GQD and ZnO NF structures. The UV (ca. 385 nm) and blue region (400–475 nm) emissions originate from the near-band-edge emission of ZnO and the excitation via recombination in the GQDs, respectively. The green–red region (500–700 nm) comprises several emission bands from different defect levels. Furthermore, blue emission is clearly observed selectively in ZQDs/ZnO NFs under illumination with 365 nm light-emitting diode by the naked eye (Fig. S4). With the aim to explain the above interesting phenomena, Fig. 5c shows the energy-band diagram of GQDs/ZnO NF structure. The PL emission of GQDs in Fig. 5(a) can be considered as a transition from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital to the highest occupied molecular orbital. One of the most interesting findings herein is the enhanced PL intensity of GQD/ZnO NF/glass as compared to those of the GQDs/glass and the ZnO NF/glass. ZnO NF with high surface-to-volume ratio can act as an excitation matrix for GQDs while enabling a high packing density of GQDs on the ZnO surface, with the PL intensity of GQDs (400–475 nm) increasing as a result. Moreover, the resonant energy transfer between the defect levels and GQDs provides a radiative recombination route for defect-related emission across the whole visible spectrum. The GQDs potentially increases this recombination rate by transferring photo-excited electrons from GQDs to the defect levels. These transferred electrons to different types of defect levels recombine with holes in the valence band, giving rise to a broad and enhanced PL emission. We also demonstrate that GQD/ZnO NF structures can be fabricated on flexible PET substrates, as shown in Fig. 5d. The PL spectrum of ZnO NF and GQD/ZnO NF structures on a PET substrate is very similar to those obtained on a glass substrate (Fig. 5b). The inset image in Fig. 5d shows selective blue emission from ZQDs/ZnO NF on the PET substrate under illumination with 365 nm light source in bending condition, which suggests that the structure can be used for application in flexible devices. The PL intensity of the GQD sample in the blue region is higher than that on the glass substrate, which can be explained by the different densities of GQDs on the ZnO NF surface when spray-coating was performed. The factors that may affect on the optical properties are the amount of the GQDs solution and the surface temperature of the substrate when spray-coating is performed (Fig. S5). The enhanced intensity of the PL spectrum originates from the highly packed GQDs on the ZnO NF surface as well as from the resonant electron transfer between GQDs and the defect levels.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated an introduction of two-dimensional ZnO NFs for more efficient integration of GQDs. The hybrid GQDs/ZnO NFs was fabricated on the hard and flexible substrates by using a straightforward solution-based technique. It allows a high distribution density of GQDs on the limited area, resulting in strong PL peak emission at around 442 nm. We revealed that the resonant energy interaction between the GQDs and various defect levels in the ZnO NFs played important roles in promoting the radiative recombination from GQDs and defect sites. Our approach has several advantages including easy scalability, tunable wavelength by modifying the ZnO nanostructure and GQDs, and applicability to flexible substrates. We have shown that the integration of GQDs with two-dimensional ZnO nanostructures can be promising for novel next-generation optoelectronic applications.

Young Jae Park: Conceptualization, Experiments, Analyses, Writing and Editing. Kang Bok Ko: Experiments, Analyses. Kyu Seung Lee: Experiments. Tae Hoon Seo: Experiments. Chang-Hee Hong: Reviewing, Editing. Tran Viet Cuong: Methodology, Experiments and Supervision. Dong Ick Son: Reviewing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by financial support from the KIST Institution Program (2Z06030), Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2017R1A2B3002307 & 2016M3A7B4900135), and Basic Science Research Program through the NRF of South Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1D1A3B03030758). We also acknowledge helpful advice from Prof. Russell Dean Dupuis at Georgia Institute of Technology (United States) and Prof. Jaehee Cho at Chonbuk National University (South Korea).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145407.
